coredata.dc:title: A BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets based fluorescence “off-on-off” and colorimetric sensor with smartphone detector for the detection of organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fang
Lei, Tiantian
Zhang, Yingli
Wang, Yaping
He, Yu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, boron carbon oxynitride quantum dots (BCNO QDs) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process of ethanolamine and boric acid. BCNO QDs exhibited blue fluorescence with the optimal excitation/emission fluorescence peak at 335 and 420 nm, respectively. As an efficient fluorescence quencher, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets can effectively quench the fluorescence of BCNO QDs via the inner filter effect (IFE). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylcholine (ATCh) to produce thiocholine (TCh). TCh can reductively degrade MnO2 nanosheets to generate Mn2+, thereby recovering the fluorescence of BCNO QDs. Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) can inhibit the activity of AChE enzymes, thereby preventing the production of TCh and the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets, resulting in the fluorescence “turn-off”. Therefore, the concentration of OPs can be detected by measuring the fluorescence intensity change of AChE-ATCh-MnO2-BCNO-QDs system. Under optimal experimental conditions, the dynamic detection range of paraoxon is 0.1–250 ng mL−1, and the detection limit is 0.03 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, the reaction system also showed concentration-dependent visual color changes from colorless to brownish. Furthermore, we prepared a portable BCNO QDs test paper. By using a smartphone to identify the RGB values of the reaction solution and the corresponding test paper, we carried out the digital image chromaticity analysis, which can shorten the detection time and reduce the detection cost, and provide an effective solution for the rapid detection of OPs on site.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BCNO QDs
MnO2 nanosheets
Organophosphate pesticides
Fluorescence analysis
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Detection procedure for AChE and OPs
Preparation of visual BCNO QDs test papers
Characterization of BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Sensitive detection of AChE and paraoxon
Visual BCNO QDs test paper for the detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21707030 ) and Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering ( SKLBEE2020017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of BCNO QDs; inset: the lattice fringe image (bottom left); the size distribution histogram of BCNO QDs (upper right). (B) XRD pattern of BCNO QDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of BCNO QDs. (D) XPS spectrum of BCNO QDs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets (black line) and the excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectrum of BCNO QDs. (B) The lifetime of BCNO QDs in the absence and presence of MnO2 nanosheets. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of BCNO QDs and BCNO QDs + MnO2 nanosheets. (D) The FL intensity of BCNO QDs, BCNO QDs + MnO2 nanosheets, BCNO QDs + AChE, BCNO QDs + ATCh, BCNO QDs + OPs, BCNO QDs + MnO2 nanosheets + AChE + ATCh, BCNO QDs + MnO2 nanosheets + AChE + ATCh + OPs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The influence of pH on the detection of BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets-AChE-ATCh system. (B) The influence of incubation temperature on the detection of BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh system. (C) The influence of incubation time on the detection of BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh system. (D) The influence of the volume of MnO2 nanosheets of BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh system. (E) The influence of reaction time with MnO2 nanosheets of BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh system. (F) The influence of inhibition time on the detection of BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh-OPs system.

(A) The FL spectra of MnO2–BCNO QDs system with various activities of AChE. (B) The linear graph of (F/F0) versus AChE activity. (C) The corresponding photographs of the BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh system taken in daylight. (D) Plot of solution color change (blue channel) versus activity of AChE. (E) The FL spectra of the AChE-ATCh-MnO2-BCNO QDs system with different concentration of paraoxon. (F) The linear graph of (F/F0) versus paraoxon concentration. F and F0 were the FL intensity of the AChE-ATCh-MnO2-BCNO QDs system in the presence and absence of paraoxon. (G) The corresponding photographs of the BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets -AChE-ATCh-OPs system taken in daylight. (H) Plot of solution color change (blue channel) versus concentration of paraoxon. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity of the MnO2–BCNO QDs-ATCh-AChE system in the presence of diverse interfering substances (25 μg mL−1). (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of the MnO2–BCNO QDs-ATCh-AChE-OPs system with the interfering substances (25 μg mL−1).

(A)Schematic diagram of detection of AChE and OPs based on BCNO QDs test paper. (B) Fluorescence color image of BCNO QDs test paper with various activities of AChE under 365 nm UV lamp. (C) Plot of fluorescence color change (blue channel) versus activities of AChE. (D) Fluorescence color image of BCNO QDs test paper with various concentrations of paraoxon under 365 nm UV lamp. (E) Plot of fluorescence color change (blue channel) versus concentration of paraoxon. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the fluorescence sensing for OPs based on BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets system.
Performance comparison of different methods for detecting OPs.
Recoveries of paraoxon spiked into different samples by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: A BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets based fluorescence “off-on-off” and colorimetric sensor with smartphone detector for the detection of organophosphorus pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials
Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, boron carbon oxynitride quantum dots (BCNO QDs) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process of ethanolamine and boric acid. BCNO QDs exhibited blue fluorescence with the optimal excitation/emission fluorescence peak at 335 and 420 nm, respectively. As an efficient fluorescence quencher, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets can effectively quench the fluorescence of BCNO QDs via the inner filter effect (IFE). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylcholine (ATCh) to produce thiocholine (TCh). TCh can reductively degrade MnO2 nanosheets to generate Mn2+, thereby recovering the fluorescence of BCNO QDs. Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) can inhibit the activity of AChE enzymes, thereby preventing the production of TCh and the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets, resulting in the fluorescence “turn-off”. Therefore, the concentration of OPs can be detected by measuring the fluorescence intensity change of AChE-ATCh-MnO2-BCNO-QDs system. Under optimal experimental conditions, the dynamic detection range of paraoxon is 0.1–250 ng mL−1, and the detection limit is 0.03 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, the reaction system also showed concentration-dependent visual color changes from colorless to brownish. Furthermore, we prepared a portable BCNO QDs test paper. By using a smartphone to identify the RGB values of the reaction solution and the corresponding test paper, we carried out the digital image chromaticity analysis, which can shorten the detection time and reduce the detection cost, and provide an effective solution for the rapid detection of OPs on site.

We established an “off-on-off” fluorescence platform for OPs detection based on BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets and prepared a portable test paper that can rapidly detect the concentration of OPs by digital image colorimetry.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of phosphorus-containing pesticides, organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) widely used in food, agriculture and other fields as insecticides, bacteriostatic agents and herbicides [1]. However, the adverse effects to ecological environment and food safety caused by pesticide residues are becoming serious [2]. As a neurotoxin, OPs can inhibit the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), making it unable to catalyze the decomposition of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ATCh) into thiocholine (TCh) [3]. The excessive accumulation of ATCh resulted in disorder of nerve conduction, causing dyskinesia and coma, DNA damage, respiratory center paralysis, and even death [4]. Therefore, exploring efficient and rapid detection of low-level OPs is essential to the protection of food safety and human health. So far, many methods have been designed to detect OPs, including capillary electrophoresis (CE) [5,6], gas chromatography (GC) [7,8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], mass spectrometry (MS) [11,12] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [13,14], etc. These methods are sensitive and reliable but some difficulties still limited their practical application, such as cumbersome sample pretreatment process, long detection time, high cost and dependence on professional technology. Therefore, it is highly desirable to establish a low-cost, reliable, simple and efficient analysis method for detecting OPs. Recently, the fluorescence-based strategy has been considered a promising method for the detection of OPs because of its high sensitivity, fast response, simplicity and easy-operation. One of the key issues in the development of fluorescence sensor for the detection of OPs is to the exploration of advanced functional nanomaterials with high fluorescence performance. So far, a lots of quantum dots QDs-based probes such as Mn–ZnS QDs [15,16], carbon QDs [17,18], CdSe/ZnS [19,20], have been established for the detection of OPs. However, the majority of QDs-based probes are subjected to poor stability, high toxicity or complex synthesis. Therefore, convenient and low-cost for synthesis of new quantum dots for rapid and in situ detection of OPs is highly desirable.

The boron carbon oxynitride (BCNO) phosphor, a new type of non-rare-earth-doped BN-based luminescent material, has attracted wide attention due to its non-toxic, high quantum efficiency and tunable emission wavelength [21,22]. The optical properties of BCNO phosphor, such as light absorption, scattering and emission, have made their huge potential applications in lighting, displays, and biological imaging [23]. However, the poor fluorescence properties in aqueous solutions hampers the further application of BCNO phosphor for biological and optical applications. The exploration of BCNO QDs is an effective way to widen their application as fluorescence sensor by improving the fluorescence yields, biocompatibility and water solubility. However, there are only a few reports focusing on the synthesis and application of BCNO QDs for sensor application. Jia et al. reported BCNO QDs using the SBA-15 template method for the detection of heavy metal ions [24]. Rong et al. synthesized of BCNO QDs by hydrogen peroxide-assisted ultrasound for the detection of anthrax biomarkers [25]. Han et al. employed microwave method to synthesize BCNO QDs and used them for anti-counterfeiting labels and confidential information encryption [26]. Therefore, much more effort should be done to adjust the fluorescence capacity of BCNO QDs and develop BCNO QDs as fluorescent nanoprobe for sensing.

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets as a new kind of two-dimensional nanomaterial, have been extensive investigated due to their high specific surface area, outstanding electron transfer characteristics, strong oxidation ability, catalytic activity [27]. MnO2 nanosheets own a broad absorption spectrum (200–600 nm) with a large molar extinction coefficient at 380 nm, which endows their strong fluorescence quenching ability to the most fluorescence materials [28]. Moreover, MnO2 nanosheets have oxidation ability and can be converted to Mn2+ by reductive agents, such as ascorbic acid, cysteine, or glutathione. According, a variety of fluorescence “off-on-off” sensors have been designed by combination of fluorescent probes and MnO2 nanosheets [29–31].

Herein, a fluorescence “off-on-off” and colorimetric test paper sensor with smartphone detector was fabricated for the fast, accurate, in situ and sensitive detection of OPs based on BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets in the principle of enzyme inhibition. As shown in Scheme 1 , MnO2 nanosheets switched off the fluorescence of BCNO QDs through the internal filtration effect (IFE). AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of the substrate ATCh to generate thiocholine (TCh). In the present of TCh, MnO2 nanosheets were reduced to Mn2+ and lost the quenching ability, accompanying with the increase fluorescence of the BCNO QDs. Therefore, the fluorescence of the BCNO QDs have a proportional restoring with the increase of AChE activity because the concentration of TCh was controlled by the hydrolysis reaction of ATCh. The OPs are well known as irreversible inhibitor to AChE activity. After addition of OPs, the generation of TCh and the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets were prevented because OPs block the AChE activity, leading to the BCNO QDs being quenched again. The senor makes it clear to distinguish colors from colorless to brownish with the increase of OPs concentration under daylight. Therefore, an efficient fluorescence “off-on-off” and colorimetric method is established to detection of AChE activity and OPs. According to the National Surface Water Environmental Quality Standard, the standard limit of OPs in drinking water is 3 ng mL−1. Under optimal experimental conditions, the dynamic detection range of paraoxon is 0.1–250 ng mL−1, and the detection limit is 0.03 ng mL−1, which meets the detection requirements of the national standard. Then, we designed a test papers platform for the detection of AChE activity and OPs based on BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets by reading the RGB value by Color Recognizer APP installed in the smartphone. Moreover, smartphone-assisted probe was successfully applied to the monitoring of OPs in the water and soil. This rapid, convenient and equipment-free strategy provides a new avenue for the detection of OPs in the environment sample.

All chemical reagents are at least analytical grade. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE), acetylcholine (ATCh), glucose oxidase were purchased from Solarbio Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethanolamine, boric acid, aspartic acid, histidine, trypsin, pepsin were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Paraoxon was purchased from Laiyao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Xinyang, China). 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES), lysozyme were purchased from Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KMnO4 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2 were purchased from Bodie Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Glycine was purchased from Yuanhang Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence spectrum was measured on an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, America). Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime were analyzed on FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, England). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured at room temperature with a Spectro-Type FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was tested using the TecnaiG20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, American). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image was measured by JEM-2100 UHR (JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption measurement was performed on a Lambda 35 UV analyzer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on the D8 DISCOVER diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, American).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL ethanolamine and 0.309 g boric acid were dissolved in 10 mL purified water, and the mixture was put into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the obtained solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 D) for 12 h to remove small molecules. Finally, BCNO QDs were freeze dried to a pale-yellow solid.

MnO2 nanosheets were synthesized according to previous reports [32]. Briefly, 1 mL KMnO4 (10 mM) and 10 mL MES (0.1 M, pH 6.0) were mixed and sonicated for 30 min. Afterwards, the obtained MnO2 nanosheets were centrifuged and washed repeatedly with purified water. Finally, MnO2 nanosheets were re-dispersed in purified water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection procedure for AChE and OPs
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations of AChE (20 μL) were mixed with 10 mM of ATCh (20 μL) and 10 mM of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, 1000 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (0.5 mg mL−1) and 200 μL of BCNO QDs (300 μg mL−1) were added to the solution. After the above solution was reacted completely for 20 min and fluorescence signals were recorded for the detection of AChE.

Different concentrations of paraoxon were added to 24 μL of AChE solution (1000 Unit L−1) for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, ATCh (20 μL, 10 mM) and PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) were added and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, 1000 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (0.5 mg mL−1), 200 μL of BCNO QDs (300 μg mL−1) were added. The fluorescence emission spectrums (excited at 335 nm) were recorded.

The photo of the solution color in daylight was taken with a smartphone (Redmi note 7 pro), and then the color information (RGB value) was obtained through a smartphone application (color recognizer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of visual BCNO QDs test papers
ce:section.ce:para: Commercial fiber filter paper was used to make circular strips with a diameter of about 1 cm. The BCNO QD-MnO2 nanosheets mixed solution (50 μL) was repeatedly dropped onto the circular strips in 5 times, and then dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain BCNO QDs test paper.

10 μL AChE solution (the concentration of ATCh is 1 mM) with different activities was dropped on the test paper and reacted for 20 min. Then, the fluorescence image was collected with a smartphone under ultraviolet light at 365 nm, and then RGB was obtained through the color recognizer.

In order to detect OPs, different concentrations of paraoxon solution and AChE solution (12 Unit L−1) were incubated for 30 min, and then ATCh (20 μL, 10 mM) and PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) were added and incubated 30 min. Then took 10 μL of the mixed droplet and reacted on the test paper for 20 min, and obtained its RGB value through the color recognizer app.

Real sample detection was conducted by investigating the recoveries of different spiking concentrations of paraoxon in soil and water samples. The water samples were collected from Shahu Lake and our laboratory (Hubei University, Hubei, Wuhan). All water samples were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and filtered with a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to improve purity. Soil sample (5.0 g) was soaked in 50 mL water for 24 h, and then centrifuged and filtered it. Then, 10 mL of sample solution was taken, and 1, 10 and 50 ng mL−1 of paraoxon was added to detect and calculate the standard recovery rate. In the experiment, each sample was measured three times in parallel, and the standard deviation (SD) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: BCNO QDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method with a mixed solution of boric acid and ethanolamine at 200 °C for 12 h. The TEM image (Fig. 1 A) shows that BCNO QDs are uniform in size, with a particle size distribution ranging from 1 to 5 nm and an average size of 2.33 nm (Fig. 1A, inset, upper right). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1A, inset, bottom left) reveals a lattice spacing of 0.213 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice fringes of h-BN [33]. The XRD pattern of BCNO QDs have an obvious peak at 26° (Fig. 1B), which corresponds to the (002) plane of h-BN and a broad peak at 43° points to the (100) plane of h-BN [34]. In order to further identify the chemical composition and structure of BCNO QDs, FTIR spectroscopy and XPS were carried out. As shown in Fig. 1C, the absorption peaks at 780 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1 are attributed to the B–N stretching and flexural vibration modes of h−BN, respectively. The absorption peak at 1050 cm−1 corresponds to the N–B–O bond, while the absorption peak near 1200−1600 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N and B–N bonds. In addition, the absorption band at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H band [35,36].

The XPS spectra of BCNO QDs (Fig. 1D) illustrates four peaks at 190.80, 284.90, 400.06 and 532.00 eV, indicating the presence of B, C, N and O elements. The B 1s spectrum (Fig. S1A) indicates typical characteristics of B–C (190.2 eV), B–N (191.0 eV), and B–O (192.4eV) bonds. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) exhibits typical characteristics of C–B (283.6 eV), C–C (284.8 eV), C–N/C–O (285.8 eV), and CN/CO (287.8 eV) bonds. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S1C) reveals typical characteristics of N–B (398.4 eV), C–N (400.0 eV), and C–NH (401.0 eV) bonds. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1D) displays typical characteristics of O–B (530.9 eV) and O–H (532.5 eV) bonds [37,38]. Based on the above results, it can be confirmed that BCNO QDs were successfully synthesized.

MnO2 nanosheets were prepared by a top-down ultrasound method. As depicted in Fig. S2A, the lamellar structure of MnO2 nanosheets can be observed, showing two-dimensional morphological characteristics. Fig. S2B shows that the UV–Vis spectra of MnO2 nanosheets generally has a wide absorption band (300–600 nm), and the characteristic absorption is around 380 nm [39]. And it can be seen from the XPS spectrum (Fig. S2C) that there are two obvious peaks at 529.6 eV and 641.9 eV, indicating the presence of O1s and Mn 2p in the sample. The high-resolution spectrum of Mn 2p (Fig. S2D) shows two peaks at 642.0 eV and 653.9 eV, which are attributed to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, respectively, confirming the presence of Mn (IV) [40]. The above characterization results are consistent with the literature reports, proving that the MnO2 nanosheets were successfully prepared. Afterwards, the BCNO–MnO2 system was observed by TEM, and the obtained images clearly show that BCNO QDs are well dispersed on the surface of the MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S3A). The zeta potential of MnO2 nanosheets, BCNO QDs and BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets system were measured. From the results (Fig. S3B), it can be seen that MnO2 nanosheets exhibit a negative potential (−20.2 mV), BCNO QDs exhibit a positive potential (3.4 mV), and the zeta potential of the BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheet system is −16.32 mV. It shows that BCNO QDs adhere to the surface of MnO2 nanosheets through electrostatic interaction.

The optical properties of the BCNO QDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As observed in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence emission peak of BCNO QDs exhibits excitation-dependent performance when the excitation wavelength increases from 270 nm to 370 nm. The fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S4B) shows that the optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of BCNO QDs are located at 335 nm and 420 nm, respectively. The inset in Fig. S3B shows that the BCNO QDs emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of BCNO QDs measured by using an integrating sphere is 20.06%. The chemical stability and photostability of BCNO QDs were studied in Fig. S4. The fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs at 420 nm was basically unchanged in the pH ranging from 6 to 8 (Fig. S5A). The fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs remained stable over salt concentrations ranging from 0 to 10 mmol L−1 (Fig. S5B). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs did not change significantly when stored in 4 °C for 2 months (Fig. S5C). The results show that the prepared BCNO QDs has good stability, making it promising to be used as fluorescence probe.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, BCNO QDs performed a maximum fluorescent signal at 420 nm under the excitation of 335 nm. The absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets overlaps considerably with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BCNO QDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the fluorescence lifetime of BCNO QDs does not change significantly before (7.92 ns) and after (7.78 ns) the addition of MnO2 nanosheets. Moreover, in Fig. 2C, after adding the MnO2 nanosheets, the UV–visible absorption peak of BCNO QDs does not change obviously, indicating that no complex was formed. According, the most likely sensing mechanism between BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets may be IFE. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs is remarkably quenched by MnO2 nanosheets through the IFE effect (violet line). When AChE and ATCh are introduced, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs can be restored (yellow line). This is because AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of ATCh to generate TCh, and TCh contains sulfhydryl groups and has reductive properties that can degrade MnO2 nanosheets, thereby restoring the fluorescence of BCNO QDs. As shown in equation (1), MnO2 nanosheets are reduced to produce Mn2+.
(1)
MnO 2
+ 2 RSH
+ 2 H
+
→
Mn
2 +
+ RSSR + 4 H
2
O

After adding paraoxon, the fluorescence of the system is quenched again (brown line). This is because paraoxon can inhibit the activity of AChE, so that AChE cannot catalyze ATCh to generate TCh, and thus cannot decompose MnO2 nanosheets. In addition, BCNO QDs were incubated with ATCh, AChE, and paraoxon respectively. As shown in Fig. 2D, the FL intensity remained almost stable, indicating that BCNO QDs are not affected by ATCh, AChE and paraoxon alone. The above results indicate that the principle of detecting OPs is completely feasible.

In order to ensure the sensitivity of detection, the influences of pH, incubation temperature, the volume of MnO2 nanosheets and reaction time on the system were investigated. First, for the BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets-AChE-ATCh system, as shown in Fig. 3 A, the FL intensity recovered to its maximum value when the pH value was 7.4. As shown in Fig. 3B, when the incubation temperature was 37 °C, the enzymatic reaction had the highest catalytic efficiency. In addition, the effect of incubation time is shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs increased linearly from 0 to 30 min, and stabilized after 30 min, indicating that the enzymatic hydrolysis reaction was basically completed. Therefore, in order to make the reaction sufficient, we choose 30 min as the reaction time. As shown in Fig. 3D, with the increase of the addition volume of MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs decreased gradually, and when the amount of addition reached 1000 μL, the decrease of fluorescence intensity was no longer obvious. Considering the savings, the volume of MnO2 nanosheets were selected to add 1000 μL. As shown in Fig. 3E, When the reaction time reached 20 min, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs remained stable. For the BCNO QDs-MnO2 nanosheets-AChE-ATCh-OPs system, as shown in Fig. 3F, when the inhibition time reached 30 min, the inhibitory effect of paraoxon on AChE reached the maximum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of AChE and paraoxon
ce:section.ce:para:
As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs gradually recovered with the increase of AChE activity from 0 Unit L−1 to 12.0 Unit L−1. The regression equation is: F/F0
=
0.17 C AChE (Unit L−1) +1.0 (Fig. 4B). And the limit of detection (LOD) for AChE is 0.3 Unit L−1. In addition, as the activity of AChE in the detection system increases, a color change from brown to colorless could be observed (Fig. 4C). We collected the color information (RGB value) of the reaction solution under different AChE activities by using a smartphone application (color recognizer). The results showed that the blue channel and AChE activities had a good linear relationship. The regression equation is: y = 7.5 C AChE + 67.3, and the LOD is 0.6 Unit L−1 (Fig. 4D). Different concentrations of paraoxon were incubated with AChE (12 Unit L−1) at 37 °C for 30 min, and then introduced into the sensor system. As shown in Fig. 4E, as the increase of paraoxon concentration, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually. Two optimum fitting straight lines between F/F0 and the concentrations of paraoxon was obtained at a concentration range of 0–75 ng mL−1 and 75–250 ng mL−1. The two regression equations are: F/F0 = 0.99–0.00067 C paraoxon, F/F0 = 0.66–0.0014 C paraoxon (Fig. 4F). And the LOD for paraoxon is 0.03 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, a series of obvious color changed from colorless to brown as the concentration of paraoxon increases (Fig. 4G). Similarly, the color information (RGB values) of the reaction solution was obtained by Color recognizer. The results showed a good linear relationship between the blue channel and the concentration of paraoxon (1–100 ng mL−1 and 100–250 ng mL−1). The two regression equations are: y = 154.4–0.65 C paraoxon, y = 103.0–0.11 C paraoxon, and the LOD was 0.3 ng mL−1 (Fig. 4H).

As shown in Table 1 , compared with other methods, the fluorescence analysis method established in this study has lower LOD and higher sensitivity, and can be used for the quantitative detection of paraoxon.

Selectivity is an important feature for evaluating the performance of probe. In order to evaluate the specificity of the method for the detection of paraoxon, the influences of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, aspartic acid (ASP), glycine (GLY), histidine (HIS), glucose oxidase (GOX), Trypsin (TRY), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), lysozyme (LZM), pepsin (PEP) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) was investigated.

As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the BCNO QDs-MnO2-ATCh system possesses a significant response to AChE, and the fluorescence intensity displayed no obvious changes after adding these common substances, proving that this strategy shows high selectivity for the detection of AChE. As shown in Fig. 5B, the BCNO QDs-MnO2-ATCh-AChE system had a significant response to paraoxon (0.25 μg mL−1), whereas the FL intensity did not change distinctly after adding 25 μg mL−1 of these common substances (100 times), indicating that the proposed strategy shows high selectivity for the detection of paraoxon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual BCNO QDs test paper for the detection
ce:section.ce:para: At present, the development of simple and convenient test paper has been widely concerned. We used commercial fiber filter paper to prepare a portable BCNO QDs test paper. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the presence of AChE made the test paper emit bright blue fluorescence, while the pre-incubation of OPs and AChE made the test paper not glow. The important thing was that changes in brightness can be seen by our eyes without any equipment, while the changes in RGB values could be quantitatively analyzed using a smartphone APP (color recognizer). As shown in Fig. 6B, the higher the AChE activity, the higher the fluorescence brightness of the test paper. Fluorescence color photos were taken by a smartphone and the RGB values of these photos were identified, and it was found that the blue channel value increased with the increase of AChE activity. And the blue channel had a good linear relationship with AChE activity (0–12 Unit L−1). The regression equation is: y = 7.4 CAChE+163.5, and the LOD calculation result is 0.6 Unit L−1 (Fig. 6C). The response behavior of the test paper to OPs was studied by dropping paraoxon-treated AChE solution with the increase of OPs concentration, the fluorescence brightness of the test paper gradually dimmed (Fig. 6D). Similar to the detection of AChE activity, the RGB values of OPs at different concentrations are also recorded. By drawing the relationship between the blue channel values and paraoxon concentrations (Fig. 6E), two good linear relationships could be obtained in the range of 0–100 ng mL−1 and 100–250 ng mL−1. The two regression equations are: y = 247.3–0.72 C paraoxon, y = 197.3–0.19 C paraoxon, and the LOD was 1.6 ng mL−1.

In order to prove the accuracy of the method, we applied the detection system to the detection of paraoxon in environmental and agricultural samples (tap water, river water, soil). Since there was no paraoxon in the actual sample, the standard addition method was used for the recovery test. As shown in Table 2 , the standard recoveries were between 92.0% and 109.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 3.5%, indicating that the established strategy has good accuracy and precision, and can be used for the detection and analysis of paraoxon in real samples.

In this work, we constructed an “off-on-off” fluorescence sensing platform for detecting OPs by taking advantage of the unique fluorescence properties of BCNO QDs and the redox reaction between MnO2 nanosheets and enzyme products. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the dynamic detection range of this method is between 0.1 and 250 ng mL−1, and the detection limit is 0.03 ng mL−1. The results of standard recovery experiments show that the method has good repeatability, high accuracy, and a recovery rate of 92.0–109.0%, which has good practical value. In addition, the RGB value of the color of the reaction solution and the fluorescent color of the test paper can be recognized through a smartphone APP, which can be used for visual semi-quantitative analysis.

Fang Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Tiantian Lei: Software, Validation. Yingli Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Yaping Wang: Validation, Resources, Supervision. Yu He: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707030) and Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering (SKLBEE2020017).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.339026.
